title: *Who Thinks Like an Economist?* - Marginal REVOLUTION
gist: Beatrice Magistro's research finds economic knowledge predicts higher support for welfare-enhancing policies like free trade and a lower discount rate independent of self-interest, and Cowen now attributes this less to wisdom and more to sociological signaling and conformity pressures.

insights.claim: Economic knowledge consistently predicts higher support for welfare-enhancing policies such as Eurozone membership, free trade, and EU immigration, independent of whether individuals stand to gain or lose from globalization.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Challenges self-interest models of political economy; economic literacy, not personal stake, drives pro-globalization attitudes, relevant to forecasting trade-policy support.
insights.quote: Economic knowledge consistently predicts higher support for welfare-enhancing policies (Eurozone membership, free trade, and EU immigration), independent on whether individuals stand to gain or lose initially from globalization.
insights.locator: That is the title of a recent book

insights.claim: Economic knowledge predicts a lower discount rate even after adjusting for years of education, linking economic literacy to greater patience and future-orientation.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Suggests economic understanding and time preference are entangled, a lens on how financial literacy might correlate with long-term saving and investing behavior.
insights.quote: Economic knowledge also predicts a lower discount rate, even after adjusting for years of education.

insights.claim: Cowen has shifted from believing economic knowledge and pro-globalization views correlate through wisdom, to thinking the effect runs more through sociological expectation, perceived obligation, conformity, and signaling pressures.
insights.speaker: Tyler Cowen
insights.so_what: Reframes elite policy consensus as partly a signaling artifact rather than pure knowledge, a cynical-but-useful lens on how expert opinion forms and shifts.
insights.quote: I now think that more of the effect, however much I may sympathize with it, runs through sociological expectation and perceived obligation, combined with conformity and signaling pressures.
insights.locator: I would say that over the years
